result:
core move: never read-then-write in app code. make decrement atomic at DB level.

option 1: conditional UPDATE (best default)
- single statement: UPDATE gift_cards SET balance = balance - :amt WHERE code = :code AND balance >= :amt
- check affected rows = 1, else reject as insufficient
- atomic, no explicit lock, handles any isolation level
- works across Postgres/MySQL/most RDBMS

option 2: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE
- lock row, read balance, decide, UPDATE, commit
- more flexible when decision logic is complex (e.g. split across multiple cards, tax interactions)
- costs a round-trip, holds lock longer, deadlock risk if you touch multiple rows in inconsistent order
- use when conditional UPDATE can't express the rule

option 3: optimistic with version column
- SELECT balance, version; UPDATE ... WHERE version = :v; retry on 0 rows
- fine under low contention, degrades to livelock under high contention on same code
- not worth it here — gift cards have low write volume per row but need correctness

pick option 1 as default, option 2 for multi-step flows.

on top of that, idempotency is non-negotiable
- every redeem carries idempotency_key (e.g. order_id + attempt_id)
- record (code, idempotency_key, amount, result) in redemptions table with UNIQUE(code, idempotency_key)
- on retry, look up first — if key exists, return stored result, don't re-decrement
- without this, network retries double-spend even with atomic UPDATE

reserve-vs-capture question
- if checkout can fail after gift card debited (card decline, timeout), either:
  a) debit at capture only, accept race risk during checkout session (small window, small volume)
  b) reserve: move amount to "held" column / separate pending row, commit or release on checkout outcome
- reserves add complexity (timeouts to auto-release orphaned holds) but prevent "gift card shown as spent mid-checkout, then checkout fails" UX
- for low-volume gift cards, (a) is usually fine. for high-value or heavy use, (b)

ordering within the DB transaction matters
- decrement gift card, insert ledger row, create payment record — all in one transaction
- if card auth happens outside DB txn (external gateway), do: reserve gift card in txn → call gateway → commit or rollback reservation based on result
- never commit gift card debit before you know card auth outcome, unless you have a compensating reversal path

common trap: two gift cards on same order
- lock them in deterministic order (sorted by code) to avoid deadlock
- or process sequentially, not in parallel

monitoring: alert on any gift card going negative. should be impossible with conditional UPDATE — if it happens, you have a bug or a missing WHERE clause.
